question_text: Explain why locations within the Arctic Circle experience 24-hour daylight during the summer solstice, but locations at 60°N do not.
correct_answer: The Arctic Circle is at 66.5°N, which is exactly where the tilt of Earth's axis (23.5°) means the Sun never sets on the summer solstice. At 60°N, the latitude is south of the Arctic Circle, so the Sun dips below the horizon each day, meaning there is darkness each 24-hour period. The higher the latitude within the Arctic Circle, the more continuous the daylight becomes.
explanation: The Arctic Circle marks the boundary where the Sun's path during summer solstice means it never fully sets. At 60°N you're still south of this boundary, so the Sun does set. The tilt of Earth's axis creates this phenomenon.
